title: Strong today, slipping tomorrow
statement: Demand Power is above category, so the brand is healthy and does not need attention.
context: A brand can still be large, famous, or above category while its equity trajectory is weakening.
explanation: Above-category strength is a useful current-health read, but it does not settle the trajectory question. Momentum tells whether the brand is getting healthier, holding, or declining.
whyItMatters: This protects teams from ignoring early erosion just because the brand still looks strong on a current benchmark.

title: Perceived Value is not a price move
statement: Perceived Value is green, so we should raise the 12-pack price next period.
context: Perceived Value is a broad brand-equity signal about value perception and premium/value defense.
explanation: Brand Doctor can say the brand has broad equity permission to support value or premium. It cannot recommend exact SKU, pack, channel, or store-level price moves.
whyItMatters: This keeps Brand Doctor in the brand-equity lane and points pricing decisions back to RGM, elasticity, pack architecture, and market tests.

title: GN is a support lens
statement: Growth Navigator Reach is weak, so we should ignore the BBE read and treat this as a media problem.
context: Growth Navigator can reveal commercial signals and conversion leaks around the BBE read.
explanation: Weak Reach is a useful support-lens signal, not a reason to discard BBE. The better read is that the team should check whether the BBE pattern is being compounded or masked by a reach constraint.
whyItMatters: This preserves BBE as the signal-reading spine while still making GN useful for action.

title: Mental Availability can explain Salience
statement: Non-buyers link the brand to few Category Entry Points, so Mental Availability may help explain a weak Salience or Demand Power pattern.
context: The source-materials archive now includes EBI / Ehrenberg-Bass-inspired Mental Availability and CEP examples for future learning content.
explanation: This is a useful hypothesis if the Mental Availability evidence is available and labeled. It should support the BBE read rather than replace it.
whyItMatters: This teaches users how How Brands Grow / Ehrenberg-Bass concepts can enrich learning while keeping Brand Doctor grounded in its own evidence.

title: Different is not the same as recognisable
statement: Different is weak, so the brand's distinctive assets must be weak too.
context: BBE Different and distinctive asset strength are related strategic ideas, but they are not the same measurement.
explanation: Weak Different says the brand lacks enough perceived distinctiveness or choice advantage in BBE. Distinctive asset strength needs asset-recognition evidence.
whyItMatters: This prevents teams from jumping to asset work without checking whether the actual issue is meaning, choice advantage, recognizability, or execution consistency.

title: AI is not the rule engine
statement: The AI gave a confident explanation, so that explanation is the strategic read.
context: Brand Doctor uses deterministic rules and configured action mappings; AI explains, challenges, summarizes, and translates.
explanation: The strategic read comes from the configured rule/evidence trace, not from AI confidence. The AI can explain the read but cannot invent a new read or override missing evidence.
whyItMatters: This is central to No Magic: users should know what is data, what is deterministic logic, and what is AI interpretation.

title: Same signal, different brand
statement: Two brands have weak Different, so they should receive the same action path.
context: A Star, Mainstream, Fighter, Aspirational, Outsider, or Specialist can show the same weak signal for different strategic reasons.
explanation: The weak metric is a signal. The right action depends on brand role, life stage, category context, momentum, and supporting evidence.
whyItMatters: This helps users understand why Brand Doctor reads the full brand context, not just a metric row.

title: A valid benchmark read
statement: This score is below category, so we should ask which benchmark is creating the concern before choosing a fix.
context: Vs Category, Ahead, and Momentum answer different signal-reading questions.
explanation: This is a good BBE reading habit. The team is not jumping straight from red to action; it is asking which benchmark is speaking.
whyItMatters: A smart user learns to ask 'red versus what?' before deciding what kind of problem the brand has.

title: Business outcome is not causality
statement: Sales fell in the performance pulse, so the BBE read caused the decline.
context: Performance reports can help frame urgency, but Brand Doctor should not claim causality without causal evidence.
explanation: A business outcome can be connected context, not proof of cause. The team can ask whether BBE, availability, pricing, media, category, or execution signals help explain the pattern.
whyItMatters: This keeps the tool useful in business conversations without overstating what the evidence proves.

title: External framework context
statement: Because a BrandZ report uses similar brand-equity language, BrandZ endorses this Brand Doctor read.
context: External reports can help teach language and context, but endorsement and source-data use require explicit approval.
explanation: BrandZ can be useful context for education, typology, and external brand-equity language. It does not endorse Brand Doctor unless an approved source says so.
whyItMatters: This lets Learn borrow useful external concepts carefully while keeping product claims clean.
